metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: monetized confidence
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Locate the break-point stake at which the user trades loyalty for personal benefit. The framing keeps the action concrete (sharing a private detail) and the counterparty close (a real friend's confidence, not an abstract group), but moves the stakes through career-opportunity territory rather than pure cash, since loyalty and money interact differently than honesty and money.
metadata.domain_anchor: Haidt MFT loyalty foundation. Cikara parochial-empathy work. No direct lab→field validation exists for this exact paradigm; flagged as a high-design-risk probe whose external validity is partly being tested by inclusion.
content_warnings: potential discomfort imagining betrayal of close friend

framing_prompt: A journalist working on a longform piece reaches out. They're writing about a topic your closest friend has direct private experience with — something your friend confided in you about, that they would not want public. The journalist offers you a fee as a 'source,' off the record; nothing under your name; the friend wouldn't be identifiable by anyone but a small inner circle who already know. No realistic way you'd be discovered.
framing_question: At what fee would you agree to share?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code 'never' as Inf for break-point analysis; report separately as 'loyalty ceiling above probe range'.
analysis.interpretation_note: Loyalty break-point is highly sensitive to counterparty closeness and visibility-of-betrayal framing. The 'no realistic way you'd be discovered' clause is load-bearing — without it, the probe measures vigilance-against-consequence rather than loyalty-under-stakes. The probe is also more emotionally loaded than cov-truth-001 and cov-allocation-001; expect higher skip rates and longer response times. Both are themselves signals.
